data.title: Buying The Fear Before It Shows Up: The PBDC Setup
data.description:
The current BDC environment is potentially being seen as the start of a broader credit unwind phase, leading to some investor apathy and sharp corrections in share prices across the board. Redemptions have picked up across a few private credit vehicles, signaling investor caution. However, both history and current data show that this is more likely an early repricing regime rather than a breakdown.

This is a market that is pricing in credit risks ahead of time, and it appears that a lot of downside is already factored in, while carry returns remain intact. I think it is a good time to start accumulating selectively for a long-term portfolio. A broader portfolio like the Putnam BDC Income ETF (PBDC) should be able to diversify idiosyncratic credit risks. And as my analysis will show, PBDC is relatively skewed toward larger, sponsor-backed platforms with more resilient underwriting; the high-beta segment exposure, where stress is likely to emerge first, is relatively low.

The risks ahead are not immaterial; the question is how well the portfolio is already pricing in those risks. The PBDC portfolio still shows a few names trading at a significant premium, but some of the core holdings, like ARCC and OBDC, are trading at a deep discount. Overall, as I had mentioned in a recent article comparing ARCC versus OBDC, the BDC trade is not all about yield maximization anymore. It is about allocating based on credit quality that is priced in.

the outlook at the moment is not to position BDC trades assuming a clean environment - investors should be thinking about credit risks as rates could stay far higher than initially expected, stressing borrowers and making refinancing harder.

Where Are We in the Cycle

The extent of early pricing in of credit risk is evident from the 1-year rolling return charts based on BIZD share prices (as a proxy for BDC returns broadly, given its longer history to fall back on). The recent rebound post some easing of the Middle East tensions has meant 1-year rolling returns are now at ~-5% (they were ~20% at the peak of the crisis that was impacting broader markets, and the chart reflects that, ending March 31, 2026). The only true drawdown bigger than this, over the past 12 years, was in 2020, when rolling returns collapsed to ~-25-40%. That was more about a liquidity shock, not just credit deterioration. Even at current rolling return levels of ~-5%, this is still one of the lowest points seen across the 12-year period - and it is not like credit risks have not surfaced in this period. The energy stress in 2015-16, tightening impact in 2018, and even some parts of 2022 did not lead to sustained capital impairment at the index level. Repricing has mostly been gradual, with income more than offsetting drawdowns.

I do not rule out 2026 being a year unseen in credit risk terms (in the past 12 years) - but that sounds extreme and is not yet showing up in non-accruals across major BDCs. In fact, the market is quite early to reprice BDCs already this time - further credit deterioration is more likely to be absorbed gradually rather than being expressed through a sharp drawdown. And therefore, waiting for the cycle lows may not work out (like in past cycles) the way the current credit risk outlook suggests - I think investors will do well to start accumulating and buying on further dips to come out with a very strong portfolio on the other side of the crisis, if any. And a diversified vehicle like PBDC could balance that exposure with core exposure in quality cycle-tested platforms while keeping some alpha possibilities by including lower weights in dislocated and heavily discounted names where credit risks are overpriced in.

The Macro Does not Signal Distress

The macro picture does not support a 12-year outlier in stress terms, something the rolling return already indicates. The SOFR has eased to ~3.7% from 5% in 2024. That shows that the earnings tailwinds have diminished and borrower financing costs are elevated. However, high-yield markets, as reflected by HYG below, show a gradual drift rather than a breakdown, indicating controlled repricing of credit risk. There is no evidence yet of a breakdown below prior stress highs. Leveraged loan markets (proxied by BKLN) remain stable, which signals that underlying asset values are holding despite rising stress. Overall, this is not a clean credit environment, and income tailwinds are diminishing, but we are not in distressed territory yet. The question is how much deterioration (versus that indicated) is already priced in.

Valuation Already Reflects Selective Stress

Valuations across a few key holdings are already reflecting risks. ARCC is currently trading at ~0.94x NAV, while OBDC is trading at an even cheaper discount at ~0.75-0.8x to NAV. Even GBDC is trading at a discount. At the other end of the spectrum, HTGC and MAIN continue to trade at a premium because of their stronger underwriting track records and capital allocation discipline. Even here, the premiums have compressed from the peak already and meaningfully. HTGC has corrected further from the data shown in Q4 below and is now trading at NAV multiples last seen in 2022, and far below the 2024 cycle peak of ~1.72x. MAIN is holding relatively well - the P/NAV chart I constructed over the past 5 quarters shows a dip in Q1 2025, but that coincided with a market wide correction due to tariff uncertainties (not purely credit repricing effects). Removing the Q1 2025 aberration, the current valuations have contracted from ~1.9x - levels seen in 2025, even in Q3.

What Drives a Rebound

Because valuations have corrected ahead of actual credit stress, stabilization in the macro environment or an actual realized credit loss environment that is far lower than that already assumed is enough for the discount gap (or multiple compression) to close in several cases. Even if multiples do not revert to prior highs (possible, because we are clearly not in peak conditions for BDCs), we could see the carry trade supporting total returns.

As of today, most of the top holdings in PBDC do not indicate reasons to account for the breakdown already being priced in. ARCC continues to report strong interest coverage (~2.2x), declining leverage, and stable non-accruals (negligible deterioration YoY) - supporting a better than anticipated borrower resilience environment. OBDC shows non-accruals at ~1.1% of fair value (not an aberration from historical trends and in fact marginally trending down). Asset sales have been reported close to marks, dispelling concerns around NAV marks.

HTCG's portfolio looks even more solid - with only ~0.1% non-accruals and all loans performing, despite its exposure in a more volatile tech and venture ecosystem. MAIN also continues to generate NAV growth, realized gains, and strong dividend coverage - indicating no underlying credit stress. OTF reports low non-accruals and strong underwriting cushions (low LTV structures).

For most of the top holdings, valuations are factoring in another story, though. Even under a simplified framework, if non-accruals were to rise to ~10% of the portfolio with ~40-50% loss severity, say, that would translate to only ~4-5% NAV impact - something already priced in for ARCC and Golub, for example (and OBDC is already factoring in much more extreme conditions). The premium names have also corrected and show the non-accruals are even lower than the discounted holdings. But in case the credit stress worsens and reaches their portfolio too, PDBC's structure should see some rotation into discounted names, where allocation is higher (a self-reinforcing setup).

Overall, I expect the macro situation to be supportive - we are seeing some easing in energy prices as the Strait of Hormuz opens up (although we may not have heard the last of that). Hopes of rate cuts and easing borrower load are opening up again, and stagflation risks are easing a bit. Financing costs are still high, but we have seen that high yield and leveraged loan markets are repricing gradually, not indicating a breakdown.

Portfolio Positioning - Quality Skewed

Despite the diversification expectations from a BDC portfolio, PBDC is concentrated at the top - with ~70% of the portfolio in the top 10 and over 50% in large sponsor-backed platforms. These are scaled players with diversified borrower pools and stable non-accrual trends historically. I have already checked valuations for ARCC, OBDC, and GBDC (more than 25% of the portfolio and half of the segment in discussion) and found this segment trading at a discount too, in anticipation of credit stress ahead of time.

The second material layer in premium BDC territory consists of internally managed platforms (like MAIN and HTGC). This layer is known for stricter underwriting and capital discipline history, and valuations have contracted reasonably from peak cycle highs. While the quality here is great, a lower weight here does not mean a suboptimal allocation in my view. This is because, the premium risks are higher here. The discounted larger players segment has a better valuation floor to rely on, and unless we see calamitous credit outcomes, the risk-reward here could actually be better than the premium segment - especially when diversified.

PDBC also allocates ~15% to higher beta venture or niche lender plays where I believe stress will hit first. Another ~6% of the portfolio is opportunistic, invested in turnaround stories - BDCs that are already stressed or heavily discounted. The position size in these two buckets is contained, diversified, and keeps upside potential intact (without overburdening the portfolio with higher risks due to significant weights).

Overall, the portfolio is deeply anchored in names that can absorb stress gradually, while only a part of the portfolio (20%) is positioned for rebound. The higher beta and stressed part of the portfolio are already trading at deep discounts - so the risk-reward is balanced at least. Looking at the condition of the loan markets as of today and the relative valuation compression in the past few months, this could be the start of an accumulation phase for BDCs for investors wanting to build long-term income portfolios. Ironically, the peak of healthy conditions in 2023-25 was not the best time to Buy PBDC (looking at valuation compression since and total returns as well). Now is close to a good time to accumulate at least (a phase where risks are being aggressively priced in but not very visible yet).

data.title: CLOs: Pressure-Tested In Q1, Built For What Comes Next
data.description:
CLOs proved their mettle in Q1 2026, outperforming most credit sectors as geopolitical shocks and rising rates rattled markets, and the best opportunities may still lie ahead.

CLOs demonstrated relative resilience in Q1 2026, outperforming most credit sectors despite a challenging macro backdrop marked by rising Treasury yields, geopolitical escalation in the Middle East and growing recession concerns in the back half of the quarter. IG-rated tranches posted positive total returns, while mezzanine tranches faced pressure. A sharp selloff in software loans, the largest sector in the leveraged loan index at roughly 10-15%, was a defining feature of the quarter, driving meaningful dispersion across CLO portfolios and reinforcing the importance of manager and security selection. During the quarter, VanEck CLO ETF (CLOI) (30-day SEC yield: 5.12% as of 3/31/2026) underperformed its benchmark, J.P. Morgan CLO IG Index, by 17bps (0.75% vs. 0.92%). Meanwhile, VanEck AA-BB CLO ETF (CLOB) (30-day SEC yield: 6.20% as of 3/31/2026) was relatively in line with its benchmark, J.P. Morgan CLOIE Balanced Mezzanine Index (-0.33% vs -0.32%).

CLOs continued to compare favorably to investment grade corporates, high yield bonds, and leveraged loans, reinforcing their role as a compelling source of income and relative value amid heightened uncertainty. Our preference for higher-rated tranches remains given tight valuations and increasing tail risks, although select shorter spread-duration opportunities lower in the capital stack are beginning to present more attractive entry points.

Market Update

Total returns were marginally positive for CLOs at the index level in March. The asset class endured its worst monthly return in the past year. However, CLOs held in better than most other asset classes. Investors navigated a confluence of headwinds including an escalating and unresolved conflict in the Middle East centered on the Strait of Hormuz, sharply rising Treasury rates, and ongoing fears of AI disintermediation. The macro backdrop was difficult across risk assets broadly. Brent crude approached $120/bbl before pulling back toward $100/bbl by month-end, the S&P 500 fell 5.0% in March, and 10-year Treasury yields remain more than 30bp above pre-conflict levels, reflecting market uncertainty about the inflationary and growth implications of a prolonged Hormuz closure. The conflict has extended into April, though broader risk markets have caught a bid on hopes of a ceasefire. Despite the difficult tone, two positives are worth noting. Fourth quarter 2025 earnings season was constructive as issuers largely beat expectations and offered cautiously positive guidance and primary market activity held up well. Functioning capital markets suggest the technical picture, while challenged, is not yet in distress territory. Two-, 5- and 10-year Treasury rates traded 42bp, 44bp and 38bp higher, respectively.

Higher rated CLOs outperformed bank loans and investment grade credit and high yield bonds during the quarter.

CLO new issue supply decreased month-over-month, totaling $17.5bn in March, compared to $20.2bn in February. Refinancing and reset activity also decreased month-over-month, with $11.1bn pricing, after $23.7bn in February. Quarterly new issue volume of over $37bn was roughly in line with Q1 2025, although slightly behind the overall pace from last year, while refi reset volumes were 40% lower than Q1 last year. Retail demand continued, albeit at a slower pace, with CLO ETFs reporting $600mn in inflows in March, following inflows of $1.5bn in February. Total CLO ETF AUM is now over $44bn.

Loan market technicals remained supportive even as demand growth slowed, because repayments outpaced net new loan supply for the month. Institutional loan issuance remained constrained as the limited number of loans trading above par brought repricing activity to a halt. Wider market spreads along with the uncertain macroeconomic outlook limited issuance activity overall. However, the primary market was not fully frozen. Another wave of large M&A and LBO transactions launched during the month, bringing total quarterly volume for these types of transactions to a multi-year high. Retail loan funds saw $3.3bn in outflows in March, down from $4.1bn in February, albeit still elevated.

The trailing twelve-month default rate within the Morningstar US Leveraged Loan Index increased 6bp to 1.44%. As measured by JP Morgan, the default rate including distressed exchanges, decreased 19bp to 3.04%. Activity has been elevated as borrowers with unsustainable capital structures endeavored to manage their liabilities and avoid the bankruptcy process through liability management exercises, keeping the “official” default rate lower than otherwise. The default rate has declined 148bp off January 2025’s high.

CLO credit fundamentals largely remain strong but were marginally softer month-over-month. The overall picture appears stable as evidenced by the trailing 3-month downgrade / upgrade ratio for loans in CLOs at 1.72, down from 2.57 last month. Market value metrics saw some improvement month-over-month, largely driven by relative strength of Software and Services loans during the month, but remain suppressed overall.

US CLO secondary spreads widened in Q1. AAA’s tranche widened by 20bps, AA’s by 18, As by 29, BBBs by 77, BBs by 161 and Bs by 136. Meanwhile, the JP Morgan Leveraged Loan Index widened 87bps and the ICE BofA US HY Index widened 47bps.

Portfolio Strategy

The borrowing rate for leveraged loan companies remains high following rate increases from central banks in 2022 and 2023. However, borrowing rates moved lower following three rate cuts from the Fed at the end of 2025. The Fed has since shifted to a wait-and-see approach and held rates steady at their January meeting given solid economic growth, a stabilizing labor market and inflation above target. Markets now see the Fed on hold this year after partially pricing in a rate hike at the beginning of the month. This is a dramatic shift from year-end where more than 2 cuts were priced in.

Valuations have improved alongside the recent increase in volatility but still appear expensive overall. Given increasing tail risks in portfolios, we continue to prefer tranche purchases higher in the capital stack. However, selective shorter spread-duration assets for lower rated credits are starting to present more attractive entry points amid increasing geopolitical tensions and signs of a K-shaped economy, where middle and low economic earners continue to demonstrate weakness. This dynamic increases tail risks in portfolios, as evidenced by the recent selloff in the software sector, and underscores the need for rigorous fundamental credit analysis in CLOs, including stressing recoveries for distressed loans. Despite feeling that IG spreads are tight, we are finding value in AAA, AA, and A rated securities. We have also seen increased dispersion between managers lower in the capital stack, which could present attractive opportunities for select purchases of lower rated paper. We also expect there to be additional bouts of volatility in the coming months and would like to maintain the ability to shift further into lower rated tranches during future periods of market weakness. We previously preferred buying in the primary market, but following the software related selloff, buying in the secondary market has become more attractive.

Outlook

For risk assets generally, oil prices remain the key driver as investors grapple with a broadening supply shock, as well as hopes for deescalation of the Iran conflict. In the near term, we expect geopolitical uncertainty to remain elevated, contributing to increased market volatility and dispersion across issuers. This environment continues to reinforce a highly bifurcated loan market, where performing credits trade at relatively tight levels while a smaller group of stressed and idiosyncratic issuers drive downside risk and increase tail risk in portfolios.

While AI fears have taken a backseat following the onset of the war in Iran, the outcome is likely to have a bigger impact on long-term performance for loan issuers. We continue to see a repricing of risk premia in credit as a result of AI disintermediation in Software and other sectors as well as continued pressure in the Chemicals sector. We have seen this play out more acutely in the private credit market given the asset class’s high exposure to Software. However, while risks in the private credit market are notable, we don’t believe they pose a systemic threat and don't expect a broader market contagion.

Combined with geopolitical risks and risks to higher inflation, lower growth, and higher long-term rates, we believe that spreads are more likely to widen in the short-term and stay wider over the medium term. Given conservative portfolio positioning at the beginning of the year, we have been and continue to be well positioned to opportunistically add both in the primary and secondary markets across the cap stack, but particularly in strategies that can add BBB and lower rated tranches. However, the bifurcation in the market is notable, particularly for lower mezzanine and equity tranches. More stressed/seasoned CLOs (lower BB MVOCs and equity NAVs) are being heavily punished versus cleaner portfolios. As such, security selection is of paramount importance, and we remain highly selective when purchasing securities lower in the cap stack. We believe our bottoms-up approach of re-underwriting portfolios puts us in a good position to take advantage of security selection as spreads widen, focusing on credit selection.

data.title: Procter & Gamble: Management Said The Worst Is Over, But Q3 Earnings May Be Key
data.description:
Introduction

The Procter & Gamble Company (PG) has had a rough year, down over 14% since February. After mixed Q2 results, the stock dipped, rallied, then dipped again.

Since February, the share price has been on a consistent decline. Impacted by tariffs and geopolitical tensions, I think the price decline in the past two months has more to do with higher inflation worries from the ongoing war with Iran.

While I do expect headwinds to persist, Q2 showed the worst is likely over and that PG could see sequential improvements going forward.

In this article, I discuss their performance, what investors should expect and look for in their upcoming earnings, and despite lingering risks, Procter & Gamble is a buy at current levels.

Previous Thesis

I last covered Procter & Gamble back in December in an article titled: Appears Undervalued - A Good Time To Nibble On This Dividend Legend.

At a forward P/E of 20.1x, PG traded below its 5-year average of 24.29x. Their A-rated balance sheet, well-covered dividend yield above 3%, and upside potential to their price target of $160 made them a buy for income investors.

Despite this, I did warn investors that continued softness in their segments could lead to lower earnings and volatility in the near term. But long-term investors should take advantage by dollar-cost averaging on any dips.

Since then, the share price has been volatile but relatively stable, down a modest 0.36%. This is in comparison to the S&P 500 (SP500), up 2.38% the past 4 months.

Performance So Far

As previously mentioned, PG is down double-digits in the past year. Since February, PG is down 14%. They reported Q2 earnings this past January and saw slight improvements from the prior quarter.

During my thesis in December, after Q1, PG managed a double beat on their top and bottom lines. EPS amounted to $1.99, while revenue was $22.39 billion. This was a beat of $0.09 and $220 million, respectively.

PG saw positive sales growth across all segments, with Beauty leading the way, up 6%. Grooming & Healthcare were up 5% and 2%, respectively, while Fabric & Baby, Feminine, and Family Care were all up 1%.

But Q2 saw EPS and revenue decline from the prior quarter. EPS was $1.88, while revenue was $22.21 billion. Results were mixed as EPS beat, while revenue missed.

The disappointing performance was due to declining margins. Both core and operating margins were down by 50 and 70 basis points. Procter & Gamble saw negative net earnings across all segments except for healthcare, up 2%.

Still, management reaffirmed its 2026 guidance. Organic sales growth is projected to be between 0% and 4%, while core EPS growth is expected to be the same.

Diluted EPS growth is expected to be between 1% and 6%. At the end of Q4'24 2024, initial guidance slated diluted EPS growth in a range of 3% to 9%. Net sales are expected to see slight growth between 1% and 5%.

This was different from the prior fiscal year, where they saw positive sales and EPS growth of 2% and 4%, respectively. While the expected growth is disappointing, the good news is that management said the worst is over.

Their CFO stated Q2 was their softest quarter. And the 3% dividend raise they conducted earlier this month could be an indication that headwinds are expected to dissipate. In my prior article, I mentioned that investors should expect a $0.03 to $0.05 increase going forward.

While headwinds persist, PG's dividend was well covered by cash flows with a 63.5% payout ratio through the first six months. With the raise, the quarterly dividend increased by approximately $0.03 from $1.0568 to $1.0885 a share.

Q3 Expectations Indicate The Worst May Not Be Over

Previously, management stated they expected improvements in the back half of the year due to investments, pricing, and product innovation.

But according to analysts, PG is expecting more headwinds. Since then, the company has seen 15 downward revisions and 0 upward. Both EPS and revenue are expected to decline from the previous two quarters.

While management stated Q2 was their softest quarter, Q3 could cause more volatility and will likely be a confirmation moment. If they do see soft earnings, their worst-quarter scenario gets pushed out. And I expect them to potentially reach a new 52-week low.

But I'm confident PG could see some stabilization in Q3. I'm expecting a beat on both, with EPS between $1.58 and $1.65 and revenue around $21 billion. Investors should look for improvements in the company's segments.

Q3 will be key. If segments continue to see challenges, this could force management to revise guidance. And lead to more price volatility and underperformance for the near to medium term.

Balance Sheet & Buybacks

A positive is that their A-rated balance sheet gives them the flexibility to continue supporting the dividend during tough times. In Q2, liquidity was robust with close to $11 billion available and $25.6 billion in long-term debt.

Roughly $11 billion of debt is due within the year, but their liquidity position was strong. PG has also been buying back shares. For the fiscal year, they expect to buy back $5 billion and return $15 billion to shareholders.

Shares outstanding declined slightly from 2.458 billion to 2.424 billion. Buybacks do show that the company is in a healthy position.

Attractive For Income, Not So Much For Growth

If you're an income investor, then PG's 3% forward yield and payout ratio are incentives to invest. But I wouldn't expect much upside, at least for another 6 to 12 months.

Procter & Gamble's forward P/E of 20.6x appears attractive, especially compared to its 5-year average of 24.14x. And although they offer solid upside right now, I believe this will hinge on the upcoming quarter.

If they manage to see vast improvements in their segments, then I see the stock potentially hitting $150-$155 by year's end. But if earnings decline and margins contract further, then investors should expect the stock to fall further and possibly hit a new low.

Risks & Takeaway

The Middle East conflict remains a huge risk for Procter & Gamble. If a peace deal does take 6 months to finalize, this will likely fuel inflation as oil and gas prices remain high.

Last month saw inflation rise 0.2% from the prior month. And the longer a deal takes, oil could surge back above $100 and stay there.

If so, I suspect this could throw a wrench in PG's turnaround and result in a weaker consumer, causing them to look to cheaper, off-brands. Ultimately, impacting PG's sales & volumes.

This means margins could contract further as sales & volumes remain muted or even negative. Going forward, Procter & Gamble is likely to see underperformance unless they see vast improvements within their segments in the upcoming quarter.

While I do maintain my buy rating for income investors and think most headwinds are priced in here, investors should keep a close eye on Q3 earnings. This will likely be a key indicator of how the price will perform for the remainder of the year.

data.title: Sodexo: The Results Proved Me Wrong (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Sodexo (SDXOF) (SDXAY) was a new and relatively small addition to my otherwise concentrated value portfolio this year. Whenever I buy a company, I plan to hold it for years and even decades, rather than weeks or months.

It's rare that I sell a stock after just a short time of holding it, but in the case of Sodexo, I did just that. I bought my shares at around €42 and sold them for €41 today. That's the power of having a margin of safety. In many cases, even if we're wrong, the mistakes aren't that costly.

Here's another important concept: when the facts change, I do change my mind. And in this case, the facts did change after the recently released half-year fiscal 2026 report. In my opinion, the report doesn't show a short-term blip that I could simply ignore, but more like systemic issues that have finally become impossible to hide. That's a huge difference.

I don't sell companies because of a bad quarter or two, but in the case of Sodexo, the problems may run deeper.

It took me some time to digest all the information, but in the end, I decided that I didn't buy into a predictable low-growth company with a juicy and safe dividend yield, but rather into a turnaround play that could take years or, in the worst case, may not happen at all. Too much risk for me, hence the downgrade to "Sell".

If Sodexo were to return to its old profitability and growth rates over the next few years, the upside could be substantial. But it's no longer the safe dividend play that I perceived it to be, and personally, I can find better opportunities out there with a clearer path to decent total returns.

So, where did I get Sodexo wrong? In my initial analysis a little over a month ago, I highlighted that the company's FCF generation had been reliable over decades. I attributed this to the long-running contracts and the high retention rates that should make cash flows quite predictable going forward. I believe this assessment is still right, but I didn't factor in that Sodexo has been underinvesting for years, and higher CapEx going forward could lead to substantially lower profits.

In general, I'm not a big fan of low-margin businesses, but in the case of Sodexo, I turned a blind eye to the 3% net margin due to those long-running and mostly inflation-protected contracts. I also ignored the first guidance cut last year, although I should have known that there might be more cockroaches in the kitchen. It was a mistake, and the H1 results quickly reminded me of my misjudgment.

A decline in profitability was expected this fiscal year, which the management labeled as a transition year. But the magnitude of the decline and the once again lowered guidance showcase that problems run deeper than I anticipated.

It's a powerful reminder that a slight decline in revenue or a small rise in costs can lead to dramatic declines in profits for low-margin businesses. As a long-term investor, unless we're dealing with a very high-quality business, it's best to stay away from low-margin businesses.

Let's quickly go through the half-year 2026 numbers to see what went wrong and why I believe the risk/reward profile has become more unfavorable despite the share price drop since my initial analysis.

Sodexo's H1 2026 Was Bad

It's not uncommon to see heightened volatility on an earnings release day. In some cases, a big drop is unwarranted, and I view it as a good opportunity to buy more shares of companies that I understand at a discount, but not this time.

On release day, Sodexo's shares initially declined by more than 16%, and my desire to buy more shares was nonexistent. The market was shocked by the report, and so was I.

Revenue came in at €12.01 billion, down 3.7%, but up by 1.6% on a constant currency basis. The underlying operating profit margin fell from 5.2% to 3.7%.

Operating profit fell by 46.2%. Net profit is down by 56.7%, and adjusted net profit fell from €450 million to €285 million, or by 36.7%. Ouch.

Slightly declining revenue had to be expected because the Euro appreciated against most currencies over the last year, and most of Sodexo's business is outside Europe. It's the dramatic margin decline that was unexpected.

Before the earnings release, management guided for slightly lower operating margins, but a decline from 5.2% to 3.7% is not "slight". And it gets worse: for the full year, the company expects an underlying operating profit margin between 3.2% and 3.4%.

Organic revenue guidance has also been cut once again, from a range of +1.5% to 2.5% down to +0.5% to +1%. Lower revenue guidance and a lower operating profit margin result in rapidly declining net profits.

Sodexo is Investing in a Turnaround

Last year, the underlying operating profit margin came in at 4.7%, so a decline to 3.2% and 3.4% is worrisome. To be fair, most of the margin decline can be attributed to investments and a review of contracts and assets.

It's fair to assume if those investments and reviews weren't made, that the decline in operations, which was a 0.50% margin decline in the half-year, would accelerate from here.

It shows that Sodexo's underlying challenges are greater than I anticipated. We're not dealing with a company that simply had a weak year or two; at this point, it's turning into a turnaround situation where the duration and the outcome are uncertain. It was Warren Buffett who said that "turnarounds seldom turn".

Labeling Sodexo a turnaround play when most of the margin decline came from investments and reviews might sound quite pessimistic. And here I have to quote Buffett again: "There is seldom just one cockroach in the kitchen". In my opinion, at this point, it's prudent to expect more bad news.

The CEO admitted that the causes are deep-rooted and long-standing. It's likely they underinvested for years, and higher investments could hurt profitability for quite some time.

I'm not pessimistic when it comes to Sodexo over the very long term. After all, it's a family-controlled business with a long history in a historically stable industry. The Bellon family has a great interest in ensuring that this business remains a source of income over the next decades, as a 50-year agreement prevents them from selling their shares. But it's fairly safe to say that over the next few years, heightened investments and debt repayments could hurt shareholder returns. I'm not waiting that long when better opportunities are all around me.

Declining Profitability Leads to Higher Debt Levels

Because of the declining profitability, the debt levels are becoming a greater burden. At the end of H1 2026, net debt stood at €3.6 billion for a net debt/EBITDA ratio of 2.7x. The company now expects to end the year above its target range of 1x to 2x net debt/EBITDA.

In my opinion, Sodexo can be considered a low-growth company. The main attraction was the dividend, which was well above 6% when I bought the shares. As a registered long-term shareholder, you can get 10% on top after holding the shares for four years. And since I tend to hold my shares for decades rather than month, it would have resulted in a 7% starting yield over the long term. Not bad.

I considered the dividend to be somewhat safe, but the half-year report changed my view.

Chances of Dividend Cut Are Rising

Given the declining profitability and the debt levels, I believe the chances of a substantial dividend cut are rising. During the conference call, management was asked what will happen to the distributions, to which they replied that it was too early to tell. Not a good sign in my view.

Sodexo paid €393 million in dividends, or €2.70 a share, last year. According to my calculations, EPS could come in at €3.17 this year. Given the elevated debt levels (compared to the depressed profits), the dividend seems endangered.

€765 million in operating profit - €140 million in interest expenses - 26% tax rate = €462.5 million net profit or an EPS of €3.17

I don't care about a year of lower profits and dividend payments as long as the long-term earnings power of a company stays intact. So, the main question remains how deep and how long the decline will be. It's a question to which I don't have a clear answer at the moment, hence the sellout.

The company expects to provide more information in July, and I will join the event, not as a shareholder but as an observer. It will certainly be interesting to watch the company from the sidelines.

Conclusion

In my twenty years of investing, I've made plenty of mistakes. Sodexo is one of them. The timing of my initial analysis couldn't have been worse, and it's a perfect reminder that low margins don't matter until they do. At the time of the purchase, the shares were already trading at single-digit P/E ratios with a large margin of safety. I am grateful that the market allowed me to offload my shares at only a modest loss.

With the half-year results, the risk profile of Sodexo has changed dramatically in my view. It is now a turnaround situation with an uncertain outcome. The dividend that I perceived to be quite safe isn't as certain as I'd like it to be.

While I believe the company has the potential to do quite well over the long term, I'm not willing to wait for a turnaround, that, in the worst case, won't ever happen. For the remaining Sodexo shareholders, I wish you the best, and I will keep covering the company going forward.

data.title: REITs Are Still Misunderstood By Most Investors
data.description:
I have invested in REITs for well over a decade and write weekly articles covering them here on Seeking Alpha.

As a result, I am regularly exchanging thoughts with other investors about REITs, and this has made it very clear to me that, to this day, the vast majority of investors still don't understand the sector properly.

In today's article, I am going to discuss the 6 biggest misconceptions about REITs that I constantly come across on this platform:

Misconception #1: Commercial Real Estate Is Imploding

The media is full of stories about how "commercial real estate" is crashing down. In reality, what is typically happening is that reporters are using "commercial real estate" as a synonym for office real estate, when in reality, they shouldn't, as commercial real estate ranges across over 20 different property sectors.

So yes, the office sector is struggling, and in many cases, values are dropping. However, the office segment represents only 3% of the REIT sector.

The vast majority of REITs invest in much stronger property sectors that continue to perform well. This includes things like e-commerce warehouses, data centers, cell towers, senior housing, or even billboards.

So no, commercial real estate is not imploding. Valuations are stable for the most part.

Misconception #2: REITs Are Low-Return, Income Investments for Retirees

REITs have performed poorly over the last 5 years as they have faced a series of unfortunate headwinds.

First, the pandemic was a big headwind for REITs as we essentially shut down the outside world, hurting many property sectors. Then came the historic surge in interest rates, which drove capital away from REITs into fixed income. Finally, REITs faced a large wave of new supply deliveries, which were the result of near-0% interest rates that followed the pandemic.

All of this led to slow cash flow growth and compressed valuation multiples:

As a result, many investors now think that REITs are low-return investments that only make sense for retirees who need safe income.

But in reality, such periods of poor performance happen across every sector, as various assets go in and out of favor.

Over the longest time period that exists, REITs have actually beaten even the S&P 500 (SPY) and tech stocks (QQQ):

Some investors try to discredit this outperformance by claiming that it is the result of declining interest rates, but this alone cannot be the case, as all asset classes benefit from lower interest rates. In fact, it could be argued that tech stocks benefited a lot more than REITs, as they earn longer-duration cash flows, and as a result of this, also enjoyed greater multiple expansion over time.

Simple math also disproves this claim. If you buy a property at an 8% cap rate, enjoy 2% average annual growth, add some leverage at a 5% interest rate, and then deduct 50 basis points annually for management, you end up with 12-15% annual returns.

Real estate is simply a very lucrative asset class, and REITs make it even more rewarding as they enjoy large economies of scale, better access to financing, and other benefits.

Misconception #3: REITs Don't Enjoy the Benefits of Leverage

Lots of real estate investors imagine that REITs don't enjoy the benefits of leverage. They point out that they can use a mortgage when buying private property but not when investing in REITs.

In reality, this is nothing more than a misunderstanding of how the stock market works.

What's traded on the stock market is the equity value, not the total asset value. It is the equivalent of your down payments when buying a property. REITs then take this equity and add debt on top of it to earn higher returns for their shareholders.

Nearly all REITs use leverage, and so investors enjoy its benefits. The difference is simply that what you see fluctuate on the stock market is your equity value, not the total asset value.

REIT leverage is even better as it comes without loan origination fees, no liability risk, and often better terms than you could obtain yourself.

Misconception #4: REIT Dividend Yields Are Too Low

Investors will often also look at the dividend yields of major REIT ETFs like the Vanguard Real Estate (VNQ) and conclude that their 3-4% dividend yields are too low.

But what these investors ignore is that these big ETFs are market-cap weighted and will typically allocate the bulk of their assets into mega-cap REITs, which happen to focus on high-growth (but low-yield) sectors like data centers, cell towers, and e-commerce warehouses. Moreover, those big REITs will typically use little leverage and retain a large portion of their cash flow to reinvest in growth.

It then isn't surprising that these ETFs only offer low yields.

But that does not mean that all REITs are low-yielding investments. Our highest-yielding REIT investment is NewLake Capital Partners (NLCP), which is offering a 12% dividend yield. Its dividend has grown consistently over the years and should be sustainable:

There are lots of good REITs offering 6-8% yields if you know where to look. Even some blue-chip REITs like Realty Income (O) will occasionally sell at a near 6% yield.

Misconception #5: Rising Interest Rates Are A Huge Issue for REITs

Investors will often also avoid REITs because they think that rising interest rates are a dealbreaker.

In reality, REITs use little leverage these days. The average LTV is only about 35%, and therefore, the impact of higher interest rates is not that significant. In fact, it may even benefit them over time by reducing the growth of new supply and resulting in faster rent growth.

This explains why REIT cash flows kept rising in 2022, 2023, 2024, and 2025, despite the surge in interest rates. The growth slowed as interest expense increased, but this is not as big a deal as investors seem to imagine.

And again here, simple math can disprove this theory. As we noted earlier, if you buy a property at an 8% cap rate, enjoy 2% average annual growth, add 50% leverage at a 5% interest rate, and then deduct 50 basis points annually for management, you end up with 14% annual returns.

Many REITs have managed to access debt at a near 5% interest rate in the past year, but even if you increase that to say 6%, your returns would still remain solid and possibly even unchanged if it then leads to faster rent growth.

So no, the higher interest rates are not a dealbreaker for REITs.

It would be different if they were heavily leveraged and had lots of low-interest-rate debt to refinance in the years ahead, but this is simply not the case.

Misconception #6: REITs Are Just Value-Traps

Finally, after years of underperformance, many investors are now convinced that REITs won't ever make a comeback. They are value-traps and should be avoided.

But history shows that recent underperformers are often the future's outperformers, as valuations drop to low levels, and eventually the narrative changes and capital flows back.

Historically, whenever REITs have traded at steep discounts to their net asset values, they have been very rewarding in the following years:

And today, their valuations are some of the lowest ever recorded:

When valuations are so low, you don't need heroic assumptions to earn good returns. That is why I am particularly comfortable allocating heavily in REITs today.

Their time will come again sometime in the future, and I think that it could be already in the near-term as more investors become more concerned about AI disruption and decide to invest more heavily in AI-proof asset classes like REITs.

I think that this could push REITs back to new all-time highs, resulting in significant upside, especially if you buy the right types of REITs today. We have positioned our portfolio accordingly at High Yield Landlord.

data.title: Stewardship Across Generations: A Stronger Model For Japan's Boards
data.description:
By Bob Herr, Zhiyuan Tao, CFA and Haruna Usui, CMA

Japanese companies favor seniority, but there may be material benefits to multigenerational boards.

Japan has made major strides in corporate governance over the past decade. Reforms have included increasing board independence and modernizing committee structures. Yet one component of Japanese boards remains relatively unchanged: age. That’s a material oversight, in our view. Corporate boards that are too monolithic could be putting a damper on profits.

Corporate boards in Japan have long been characterized by seniority and continuity against a backdrop of lifetime employment. More than 95% of directors in the TOPIX 100 are in the bubble generation or older, while fewer than 1% are under the age of 50. This level of experience provides stability and institutional knowledge, but it may also entrench decision-making and hinder capital efficiency.

Many boards in Japan prioritize balance sheet safety over returning capital to shareholders and taking calculated risks. This has helped contribute to a more than 10% gap in return on equity (ROE) between Japanese and US equities. In a market long challenged by poor capital allocation, we think multigenerational boards can help buck this trend.

Multigenerational Boards Can Boost Performance

Studies in both the US and Europe present a clear link between multigenerational boards and financial performance. He, Miletkov and Staneva found that companies with younger directors not only generate higher return on assets but also command higher price-to-book values—particularly for firms that invest more in R&D and engage in patenting activity.

Younger boards can also mean less exposure to defaults—and chicanery. Janahi, Millo and Voulgaris discovered that banks with multigenerational boards experience fewer nonperforming loans, while Neukirchen, Posch and Betzer observed less corporate misconduct among firms with a greater age range.

These findings cumulatively suggest that multigenerational boards have the potential to improve capital allocation, reduce risk and boost valuations.

Mind the Gaps: Age and ROE

Our own in-house research confirms these findings. We tracked TOPIX constituents over a 10-year period—the largest study of its kind. The results were striking.

Firms with a more than 30-year age gap between the youngest and oldest director—what we define as multigenerational boards—delivered ROE more than 200 basis points higher, on average, than companies with more senior boards. This outperformance occurred in every calendar year during the period, and the results were statistically significant across sectors. Multigenerational boards achieved superior ROE in all but one sector, with the dispersion independent of size, style or founder-led status (Display).

Why the improved performance under multigenerational boards? We theorize that younger directors counterbalance the risk-averse tendencies of more senior directors. Prior academic research shows that younger boards exhibit greater risk tolerance, on average, than their senior counterparts. This is reflected in increased M&A activity and lower cash balances.

Of course, experience and qualifications remain critically important to board appointments. We favor a balanced approach that preserves legacy institutional knowledge while opening the door to next-generation dynamism.

What does this look like in practice?

Hello Kitty’s Generational Glow-Up

Sanrio (SNROF), the company behind the popular Hello Kitty brand, has long held a rich portfolio of valuable intellectual property. But historically, its business model focused largely on domestic merchandise sales. The company maintained consistent leadership for many decades under its founder, Shintaro Tsuji, which contributed to stability but slowed strategy development. As market conditions evolved and retail trends shifted, Sanrio’s merchandise business came under pressure, and the company recorded a loss in fiscal year 2020.

Recognizing the need to adapt, the 92-year-old Tsuji turned leadership of the company over to his 31-year-old grandson but remained on the board. Sanrio’s young new president initiated sweeping reforms by refreshing the board and management, overhauling retail operations, and recruiting external talent with diverse backgrounds.

The company also expanded its international footprint and leveraged social media and streaming services to amplify its brand. These changes unlocked the global potential of Sanrio’s character portfolio and repositioned the firm as an entertainment-focused intellectual-property enterprise, rather than a traditional retailer.

The effect on operating profit and ROE has been dramatic (Display). Sanrio now expects operating profit of ¥75.1 billion in FY 2025—more than triple its previous peak of ¥21 billion in FY 2013. Moreover, the company’s stock price has increased tenfold, reflecting renewed investor confidence in its strategy and earnings potential.

Sanrio’s transformation illustrates how a fresh generational perspective can address underlying issues hindering shareholder returns. In our view, if properly implemented, multigenerational boards can unlock organizational agility, greater independence and new pathways for value creation. In an era of rapid market and technological change, we believe boards that balance experience with fresh perspectives can be catalysts for building shareholder value.

The authors would like to thank Landon Shea, Investment Stewardship Associate and Research Lead, for his contribution to this piece.

The views expressed herein do not constitute research, investment advice or trade recommendations, do not necessarily represent the views of all AB portfolio-management teams and are subject to change over time.

References to specific securities discussed are for illustrative purposes only and should not to be considered recommendations by AllianceBernstein L.P. It should not be assumed that investments in the securities mentioned have necessarily been or will necessarily be profitable.

Original Post

data.title: Macro Insights: The 'Spoils Of The Iran War' Hidden In The Market
data.description:
Introduction

Welcome to the SA’s The Macro Brief! This official SA Profile will highlight our analysts' latest economic and market analysis to help investors gauge the ever-volatile financial landscape through various recurring series.

The economy is the foundation of financial markets, influencing everything from corporate earnings and consumer spending to central bank policy and inflation. Understanding economic trends, policy decisions, and sector activity can be critical when assessing market opportunities and making informed investment decisions.

Check out these must-reads from April’s first half…

Trending Themes

The Iran Conflict and the Hormuz Siege

Sell The Rally - There Is No Easy Way Out Of Iran War | Damir Tokic | 04/01/26

“The stock market bounced, driven by Iran war headlines, but the bounce is likely to be short-lived, as the macro situation remains vulnerable. The endgame for the Iran war is still uncertain, and oil prices are likely to remain high even if the war “freezes,” which seems to be the current situation. Negotiations are likely to fail, while the US seems unable to bear the economic and political costs of a ground invasion.”

3 Reasons Why There Will Be No Ceasefire In Iran | Robert & Sam Kovacs | 04/06/26

“Markets are clinging to hope of a ceasefire, which seems structurally impossible. The markets remain complacent about energy and its outlook; pricing reflects hope rather than market structure. Here are three reasons why there will be no deal by tomorrow.”

Why The U.S. Blockade May Be The Move That Ends The War | Deep Value Investing | 04/13/26

“It is my view that the U.S. blockade of Iran-bound commercial ships is mainly about regaining leverage for dismantling Iran’s nuclear program and reopening the Strait of Hormuz.”

Iran: A Game Theory Analysis Of The Hormuz Siege | Brendan O'Boyle | 04/15/26

“Markets are underestimating the risk of a prolonged Strait of Hormuz (SoH) closure, which threatens 11% of global oil supply and could trigger recession. Current equity valuations reflect optimism for a quick SoH resolution, but game theory and history suggest a drawn-out, attritional conflict is likely.”

Market Dynamics

A Major Market Rotation Is Likely Coming - Here's Where I'm Loading Up | Samuel Smith | 04/02/26

“The first quarter of 2026 was one of the most headline-whiplash quarters investors have experienced in years, with numerous forces driving market rotations…”

Why It May Be Time To Load Up Soon | Victor Dergunov | 04/03/26

“Current market volatility is driven by geopolitical risks, technical breakdowns, and sentiment, decoupling equities from fundamentals…A risk-on environment is expected to return post-Iran conflict, potentially driving 50-100% appreciation in select high-quality equities over 12-18 months.”

This Is A Most Compelling Buying Opportunity | Bill Gunderson | 04/06/26

“I believe the market is forming a major bottom, presenting a compelling buying opportunity despite recent volatility. Earnings growth remains robust, with S&P 500 Q1 2026 estimates at 13.2% and revenue growth at 9.7%, defying bearish sentiment. The forward P/E for the S&P 500 has compressed to 19.8, below its five-year average, while earnings estimates are at record highs.”

This Rally Won't End Well | James Foord | 04/15/26

“The S&P 500's resilience near 7,000 is driven by hedged positioning, CTA flows, liquidity tailwinds, and continued AI trade momentum. Despite supportive flows, I expect significant downside in the next two to three months due to geopolitical risks, persistent inflation, and adverse seasonality.”

Post-Conflict Plays

Finding The Opportunities After The Selloff And End Of The War | D.M. Martins Research | 04/03/26

“After a few false starts, enough signs have emerged recently that, certainly without any guarantees, we may be approaching the end of the most recent conflict in the Middle East-at least the uglier part of it… It begs the question: if or when the Iran war overhang subsides, where might the investment opportunities lie?”

Conclusion

We hope our analysts' insights serve as a valuable financial compass for investors seeking to make informed investment decisions.

Thanks for reading. Tell us what you think by commenting below! If you'd like to see more, follow this account.

Please let us know if you have any suggestions you’d like to see added to this series and any ideas for new additional recurring series. We're always open to your valuable feedback.

data.title: Zscaler: Strong Double-Digit Growth Ahead
data.description:
By Nicholas Tan, Investment Research Analyst @ Khaveen Investments

In this analysis, we start our coverage on Zscaler (ZS), a cybersecurity company that offers a cloud-based security platform to protect companies against cyber threats. From 2015 to 2025, its growth has been strong, climbing from $54 million to $2,673 million, with an average of 48.19%. Despite its strong historical growth, its growth rate has been slowing from 56.1% in 2021 to 23.3% in 2025. We therefore assess whether the company could sustain its high growth. We first analyze whether the company has any competitive advantage. We then evaluate whether the company could sustain its market share growth with its early mover advantage. Lastly, we assess whether the company could sustain its ARPC growth through its upselling strategy.

Strong Competitive Advantage

To assess whether the company has any competitive advantage, we compile both its competitors’ and its own competitive metrics for Security Service Edge [SSE]. We also further rank these companies' metrics to evaluate which company performed the best. Based on the table, the company ranks highest for both Malware Block Rate and Malware Evasion Resistance, showing that the company’s SSE product is more competitive than its competitors in protecting against malware. The company also ranks the highest for both Exploit Block Rate and Exploit Evasion Resistance, indicating that its SSE product is also competitive in protecting against exploits. All the companies also tie for Transport Layer Security [TLS] and Secure Sockets Layer [SSL], showing that all companies can successfully inspect and decrypt traffic that has prohibited content. The company overall performed the best out of its competitors, as it ranked first across all metrics.

We believe one key reason for its high performance could be attributable to its internal R&D. Based on our analysis, we found that Zscaler’s 10-year average R&D % revenue of 23.3% is higher than Palo Alto’s (PANW) (21.9%), Fortinet’s (FTNT) (12.8%), and Cisco’s (CSCO) (13.5%), but lower than Cloudflare’s (NET) (27.8%). While Zscaler’s 10-year average R&D % revenue is lower than Cloudflare’s, Cloudflare is a Content Delivery Network [CDN] provider, whereby Cloudflare focuses on delivering content across servers rather than focusing fully on Zero Trust.

We previously highlighted that Cloudflare has the 2nd highest market share by revenue in the CDN market and the highest market share by CDN usage of the entire internet, which suggests Cloudflare’s R&D is spread across both CDN and Zero Trust, rather than specifically on Zero Trust architecture as Zscaler is.

We thus believe Zscaler’s high internal R&D to improve Zscaler’s Zero Trust architecture could explain Zscaler’s high performance metrics. Zscaler also has an in-house R&D team, ThreatLabz, to research “emerging security issues” and “eliminate threats across” its cloud platform. Importantly, Zscaler uses the threat intelligence generated by ThreatLabz to review its products across the product development cycle, which we believe has contributed to its high-performance metrics. Apart from internal R&D, Zscaler’s customer base consists of “approximately 40% of the Forbes Global 2000 and over 45% of Fortune 500 companies”. Due to its large customer base, we believe this enables them to gain access to large volumes of data to train their models. In its Q2 FY2026 earnings transcript, management also indicated that it is “processing more than 500 billion transactions every day, more than 20x the number of daily Google searches. This proprietary anonymized data is used to train our AI engine”.

We determine that the company is highly competitive, and we further expect the company to maintain its competitiveness due to its high internal R&D spending. We believe its high R&D spending would continuously improve its Zero Trust architecture and sustain its long-term competitiveness. Based on its 2025 annual report, the company also indicated that they plan to continue investing in R&D, as it's one of its core strategies, which we believe bodes well for its long-term competitiveness in the market. While the company did not release its product roadmap, it has been releasing service updates for its core products, which are Zscaler Internet Access [ZIA], Zscaler Private Access [ZPA], and Zscaler Digital Experience [ZDX]. Year-to-date, the company released 46, 19, and 6 service updates for ZIA, ZPA, and ZDX, respectively. We believe these large service updates reflect the company’s drive to boost its product competitiveness. Thus, as the company continues to release updates for its core products, we believe that the company can sustain its long-term competitiveness in the market.

Limited Market Share Growth From Early Mover Advantage

After determining that Zscaler can sustain its competitiveness, we then assess whether the company can sustain its market share growth with its early mover advantage. We see that from 2015 to 2021, its customer growth has been strong, as its customers climbed from 2,050 in 2015 to 5,600 in 2021. This can be attributed to its first-mover advantage, as Zscaler released its Zscaler Internet Access in 2008, which featured Zero Trust Architecture and was much earlier than all of its competitors in our competitive analysis table above to release Zero Trust. For example, Cisco (2018), Skyhigh Security (2022), Cloudflare (2020), Fortinet (2021), Palo Alto Networks (2021), and Versa Networks (2023) all released their products later than Zscaler. Zscaler’s founder also mentioned that he “started Zscaler with the notion that certain users should be able to access only certain applications” and also that “the name zero trust did not exist at that time, but we are excited to see that the market has picked it up”, showing the company’s early product development of Zero Trust.

We believe that Zscaler’s earlier release of Zero Trust architecture enabled the company to sustain high customer growth from 2015 to 2021, as most of Zscaler’s competitors have not yet released their respective Zero Trust architecture. Thus, enabling Zscaler to compete in a market with no direct Zero Trust product substitute to sustain Zscaler’s high customer growth. However, recently, Zscaler’s customer growth has slowed from 24.44% in 2021 to 8.67% in 2025, which we believe could be due to the higher competition, as all of Zscaler’s competitors have released their Zero Trust products, and there are now more substitutes for Zscaler’s products. Zscaler has also now lost its early mover advantage, as all its competitors have released their products. Despite losing its early mover advantage alongside the higher competition in the market, Zscaler’s market share has been growing robustly from 6.61% in 2021 to 15.81% in 2025, which we believe can be attributable to its high competitiveness, whereby the company’s products rank best against its competitors.

The key strength of its Zero Trust architecture is that it connects users directly to internal applications without bringing users to a corporate network or connecting users to the internet without downloading web content. This importantly prevents “lateral movement” in the network, whereby hackers can gain access to the whole network if they have breached part of the network. Historically, companies used legacy architectures, such as castle and moat security, which granted users access inside the network once users had passed through internal VPNs and firewalls, or hub and spoke networks, whereby devices are routed through one central device (hub) that controls all traffic. However, these legacy architectures are more vulnerable to cybersecurity attacks, as they enable lateral movement for hackers in the network to steal data. Zscaler’s Zero-Trust architecture is more likely to prevent hacking by directly connecting users to apps and the internet without downloading web content.

We overall do not believe its early mover advantage would sustain its market share growth, as all of its competitors have adopted Zero Trust architecture, which reduces the impact of its early mover advantage. However, we do expect the company to sustain both its market share and customer growth, as we believe that the company will maintain its high internal R&D spending to enhance its Zero Trust architecture. We believe this would position its products better than its competitors and sustain its market share and customer growth. We further expect the growth drivers of the Secure Web Gateway market, such as the expected rise of cybercrime costs (2025 to 2029F, 11.01% CAGR), “increasing complexity of web-based threats”, and growing use of cloud-based cybersecurity to support its customer growth.

Upselling Strategy To Sustain ARPC Growth

After determining that Zscaler is unable to maintain its market share growth using its early mover advantage, we evaluate whether the company’s upselling strategy could sustain its ARPC growth. We thus compile its historical ARPC by dividing revenue by customers. Based on its 2025 annual report, the company uses a “land-and-expand” growth strategy, whereby they sell additional solutions and premium bundles with more functionality to existing customers. This shows that the company’s strategy is indeed focused on upselling. Based on the table above, its ARPC growth has been strong, climbing from $0.026 million in 2015 to $0.284 million in 2025, with a high average growth rate of 27%. Recently, however, its ARPC growth has been slowing from 35% in 2022 to 13% in 2025, which could be due to higher competition, as all its competitors (from the competitive analysis table) released their Zero Trust products between 2020 and 2023.

Based on its investor presentation, the company’s upsell strategy for its customers includes selling ZIA, Data Protection, ZPA, and ZDX together to achieve $175 in ARR per user. ZIA and ZPA complement each other, as ZPA directly connects users to internal apps, while ZIA safely connects users to the internet. This enables users to more safely access both the internal and external networks, which highlights ZIA and ZPA’s complementary product strengths across these networks. Whereas without one or the other, the user would be unable to either safely browse the internet or access internal applications. To add on, ZDX works well with customers who purchase both ZIA and ZPA for their users, as ZDX enables customers to track network performance across both ZIA and ZPA users. Whereas if customers purchase only either ZIA or ZPA, ZDX would be less effective, as it's only monitoring either internal apps or the internet. For users with both ZIA and ZPA, the data protection product can encrypt and protect data across both internal and external networks, making the product more effective compared to users with either ZIA or ZPA only. In terms of workload protection, workload communication connects users securely to the cloud and its services, while workload segmentation prevents lateral movement if users on the cloud become compromised. Users doing workloads on the cloud can also benefit from cloud security posture management [CSPM], as the product proactively detects and monitors cloud risks. We believe that upselling these highly complementary products is a key reason for its high ARPC growth, as customers benefit more from purchasing all these products together for its users, rather than purchasing standalone products.

* Represents the average of other competitors' products, due to a lack of data.

To assess whether its products, features, and pricing could support its future ARPC growth, we compile both its competitors and Zscaler’s Zero Trust service’s number of products, features, and pricing. We further rank these companies to analyze Zscaler’s performance. Based on the table above, the company has the highest number of products and the 2nd highest number of features. We believe this shows that the company has more product and feature upselling opportunities than its competitors to sustain its high ARPC growth.

We overall expect that the company’s ARPC growth will be strong, driven by its upselling strategy for products that are complementary to each other, positioning the company better to upsell more products to customers. Its investor presentation also shows that there is more than a “6x upsell opportunity on ZIA and ZPA alone with existing customers”, which shows that as customers adopt their products, their potential ARR with existing customers could increase by 6x, which we believe bodes well for their ARPC growth. In its Q4 2025 earnings transcript, the company also highlighted that “logos, sitting at about 45% of Fortune 500 or nearly 40% of Global 2000…… the platform is so big that we can keep on upselling and upselling”, which shows both the company’s optimism in upselling products to its large customer base and the large upselling opportunity. We also expect the company to continue developing more features for its products, enabling the company to upsell more features to existing customers. As we highlighted above, we believe that they would maintain high internal R&D spending, as it is one of their core strategies.

Risk: High Competition

We believe one of the key risks that could negatively impact the company’s future competitiveness is the high competition in the Secure Web Gateway market. Recently, Fortinet released its FortiOS 8.0 with newer features to improve its competitiveness, such as multipath IPsec tunnels, which use multiple tunnels for more reliable and faster secure connections, as well as SASE outpost, which deploys a point of presence [POP] locally in “customer-controlled locations, such as on-premises, private data centers, or co-location” for lower latency. Versa Networks also released new product updates, such as Sovereign SASE to keep data within a country or region for regulatory compliance, Secure Enterprise Browser to better protect users on the web, and also Inbound SSE to “inspect and secure inbound internet traffic”. As competitors update their OS and increase the number of features, this increases the competition in the market and could weaken Zscaler’s long-term competitiveness.

Valuation

In terms of forecasting, we first forecast its 2026F revenues using the average management’s guidance of “$3.309 billion to $3.322 billion”. We then project customer additions using its historical 3-year average, as customer growth has been slowing over the last 3 years, and we conservatively estimate that future customer additions would reflect the average of slowing customer additions. After calculating its total number of customers from 2026F to 2028F, we then derive its 2026F ARPC by dividing its revenue by the total number of customers. For 2027F and 2028F ARPC, we use its historical 2-year average, as its ARPC has slowed much more significantly in 2024 and 2025, declining from 29% in 2023 to 19% in 2024, and to 13% in 2025. As such, we projected its ARPC growth using its historical 2-year average to reflect the slowdown in ARPC. We then taper down its ARPC growth for 2028F to discount potential future uncertainty.

Based on our valuation of the company using a DCF model, incorporating our revenue projections at a 5-year forward average growth of around 22.3%, a discount rate of 10.2% reflecting the company’s WACC, and a terminal value based on the top Cybersecurity companies' average EV/EBITDA multiple of 25.29x, our model indicates a modest upside of 14.18%.

Verdict

We overall determine that the company can sustain its high revenue growth, driven by internal R&D spending and its upselling strategy. We believe that the company would continue developing new products and features as well as maintain its high internal R&D spending, which would enable the company to sustain its long-term competitiveness in the market. We further expect that this would enable them to sustain their customer and market share growth, as their products would remain more competitive than their competitors. We also believe that the growth drivers of the Secure Gateway Market, such as higher expected cybercrime costs and more complex cyber attacks, will also improve its growth outlook, as this would increase the demand for Secure Gateway Products to protect against these cybercrimes.

We further believe that the company’s upselling strategy would continue to improve its ARPC growth, as its products are highly complementary. Alongside its large customer base of 45% of Fortune 500 or nearly 40% of Global 2000 and many products and features, we believe that the company will be able to continue selling its products to customers. Based on our DCF valuation, we rate the company as a Buy with a price target of $139.56. This is despite a 44.59% decline in stock price from January 2nd, 2026 to April 9th, 2026, which made the stock significantly cheaper. We believe this discrepancy could be because its valuation ratio was previously stretched, as its 5-year average TTM P/S of 22.16x is much higher than the top Cybersecurity companies’ 5-year average TTM P/S of 7.89x, excluding outliers.

Recently, its P/S ratio declined from 16.49x on 31 July 2025 to 6.42x in 9th April, 2026 alongside a 57.20% stock price decline across this period. We believe this shows that while its stock price has become cheaper, the company was previously very expensive compared to its competitors, which mitigated the positive impact of lower stock prices on valuation.

data.title: What Moved Markets This Week
data.description:
Listen on the go! A daily podcast of Wall Street Breakfast will be available by 8:00 a.m. on Seeking Alpha, iTunes, Spotify.

Up for a challenge? Test your knowledge on the biggest events in the investing world over the past week. Take the latest Seeking Alpha News Quiz and see how you stack up against the competition.


Wall Street’s major averages posted big weekly gains, surging to new highs for three consecutive trading days, as positive developments in the Middle East—including a temporary ceasefire deal between the involved countries—raised investors’ enthusiasm.

Iran’s regime declared the Strait of Hormuz is “completely open” for all commercial vessels on Friday for the rest of the 10-day ceasefire in Lebanon. U.S. President Donald Trump, however, said the naval blockade on Iranian ports will remain in effect. He also said that Iran agreed to suspend its nuclear program indefinitely.

Both countries are expected to return to Pakistan next week for a second round of peace talks, media reports indicated, citing two senior Pakistani officials involved in mediating the peace deal. Other reports have deemed the Strait of Hormuz “remains effectively closed,” despite Iran’s announcement.

Crude oil futures (CL1:COM) decreased around 11.4% this week to settle around $85, and Brent futures (CO1:COM) also dropped 3.5% to $91 per barrel as of post-market Friday.

In economic news, both the March U.S. Producer Price Index and core PPI, which excludes foods and energy, came in below consensus. In addition, U.S. export prices climbed faster than U.S. import prices in March, according to the Bureau of Labor Statistics. The Philadelphia Fed Manufacturing Index, on the other hand, unexpectedly rose in April.

The S&P 500 (SP500) accomplished its first close above 7,100 on Friday, and the Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) extended its winning streak to 13 consecutive days, which is the longest since 1992.

For the week, the S&P (SP500) gained 4.5%, while the tech-heavy Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) rose 6.8%, and the blue-chip Dow (DJI) added 3.2%. Read a preview of next week's major events in Seeking Alpha's Catalyst Watch.

What bubble?

The AI industry continues to draw billions of dollars in funding, with investors shrugging off fears of a potential bubble and doubling down on the burgeoning technology. Investors poured $300B into 6,000 startups globally in Q1 2026, more than double the previous quarter. And most of these investments disproportionately went to AI startups, particularly in the U.S. Read more.
